case_name: Nazar Singh vs State Of Punjab And Others
court: High Court for the States of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh
citation: CWP No.12064 of 1994; CWP No.2373 of 1995

compensation_breakdown.claimant_or_victim: Dependents of the two deceased and two injured persons (claimants before MACT)
compensation_breakdown.notes: MACT award dated 2.6.1986 for Rs.3,10,427.95; High Court quashed employer's recovery order against the driver (24.2.1994) but did not modify the MACT award amount.

facts: On 12.2.1985 a Punjab Roadways bus (No. PBM-6964) driven by petitioner Nazar Singh collided with a Matador near village Mander; two occupants of the Matador died on the spot and two others were injured. Dependents of the deceased and the injured filed a claim before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT), Ludhiana. The MACT, by award dated 2.6.1986, found the petitioner negligent and awarded compensation totalling Rs.3,10,427.95, which the employer (State) paid. The employer issued a show cause (27.11.1992) and an order dated 24.2.1994 (Annexure P-3) seeking recovery of Rs.3,10,427.95 from the petitioner; the petitioner challenged that recovery in writ petition CWP No.12064/1994 (and a related petition).
arguments_claimant: The petitioner-driver contended he was not negligent or rash, denied liability to pay, and argued that the State had wrongly availed exemption under Section 146 without establishing the statutory fund as required. He also argued that no departmental inquiry was held, he was not given an effective opportunity of hearing, and therefore recovery order was invalid.
arguments_respondent: Respondents (State) relied on the MACT award finding negligence and argued the employer had a right to recover the compensation it paid from the negligent employee under the principle that an employer can recover loss occasioned by its employees' fault. They relied on precedent (HC Dilbagh Singh) to support recovery from the driver.
ratio_decidendi: "Order (Annexure P-3) has been rendered without referring to proviso to Section 146 of the Act..." The Court held that exemption from insurance under Section 146(3) is conditional upon establishment and maintenance of a fund; failure to have such a fund is a statutory violation by the State. The Court further held that "in absence of indictment in departmental proceedings, impugned order (Annexure P-3) loses its teeth, rendering it to be of no legal significance," and that an MACT award "standing alone... would not constitute the basis for recovery from the petitioner" without a proper departmental inquiry giving effective opportunity of being heard. Finally the Court emphasized that the Motor Vehicles Act is "beneficial legislation" where strict proof of guilt is not the object, but that does not absolve the employer from following required inhouse/disciplinary procedure before fixing liability on an employee.
final_order: Writ petition allowed; order dated 24.2.1994 (Annexure P-3) directing recovery of Rs.3,10,427.95 from the petitioner is quashed. The Court directed the State to insure vehicles or establish and maintain the statutory fund under Section 146 and to follow departmental procedure before effecting recoveries.
summary: This is a Punjab & Haryana High Court writ challenging the State's order recovering MACT-ordered compensation from a bus driver. The MACT had found the driver negligent and awarded Rs.3,10,427.95; the State sought to recover that sum from the driver without holding an effective departmental inquiry or establishing the fund required by Section 146. The Court quashed the recovery order, holding that an MACT award alone cannot be the basis for recovering from an employee without following departmental procedure and that the State must comply with Section 146 conditions.
embedding_summary: Nazar Singh v. State of Punjab & Others, High Court of Punjab & Haryana, Dec 21, 2013, CWP Nos.12064/1994 & 2373/1995. Facts: bus PBM-6964 (Punjab Roadways) accident on 12.2.1985 at village Mander; two deaths and two injuries; MACT Ludhiana award dated 2.6.1986 of Rs.3,10,427.95. Issues: Section 146 Motor Vehicles Act 1988 exemption/fund requirement, vicarious liability and recovery from employee, adequacy of departmental inquiry/natural justice before recovery. Court relied on State of Maharashtra v. Shirke and HC authority Dilbagh Singh; held that exemption under s.146(3) requires establishment of a fund, MACT award alone cannot justify recovery from employee without an inquiry, and quashed the 24.2.1994 recovery order (Annexure P-3). Outcome: recovery order against driver quashed; MACT award amount Rs.3,10,427.95 remains the compensation figure; Court directed State to insure vehicles or establish the statutory fund.
legal_principles: Exemption under Section 146(3) Motor Vehicles Act permissible only if a fund is established and maintained to meet third-party liabilities
An MACT award alone cannot be the basis for recovery from an employee; employer must hold a departmental inquiry affording effective opportunity of hearing
In absence of departmental indictment or fair hearing, an order to recover compensation from an employee is invalid ('loses its teeth')
Vicarious liability of the State is qua the claimants (to pay compensation) and not automatically qua the employee (to make employee personally liable) without procedure
Motor Vehicles Act is beneficial legislation where strict proof of guilt is not required for compensation claims, but employer procedural safeguards still apply
Employer may recover loss occasioned by employee's fault only after following prescribed disciplinary/recovery procedure
cases_cited: State of Maharashtra and others v. Kanchanmala Vijaysing Shirke and others, (1995) 5 SCC 659
HC Dilbagh Singh v. State of Punjab, 2002(1) RCR (Civil) 801
bm25_text: Nazar Singh vs State Of Punjab And Others High Court for the States of Punjab and Haryana at Chandigarh 2013 On 12.2.1985 a Punjab Roadways bus (No. PBM-6964) driven by petitioner Nazar Singh collided with a Matador near village Mander; two occupants of the Matador died on the spot and two others were injured. Dependents of the deceased and the injured filed a claim before the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT), Ludhiana. The MACT, by award dated 2.6.1986, found the petitioner negligent and awarded compensation totalling Rs.3,10,427.95, which the employer (State) paid. The employer issued a show cause (27.11.1992) and an order dated 24.2.1994 (Annexure P-3) seeking recovery of Rs.3,10,427.95 from the petitioner; the petitioner challenged that recovery in writ petition CWP No.12064/1994 (and a related petition). The petitioner-driver contended he was not negligent or rash, denied liability to pay, and argued that the State had wrongly availed exemption under Section 146 without establishing the statutory fund as required. He also argued that no departmental inquiry was held, he was not given an effective opportunity of hearing, and therefore recovery order was invalid. Respondents (State) relied on the MACT award finding negligence and argued the employer had a right to recover the compensation it paid from the negligent employee under the principle that an employer can recover loss occasioned by its employees' fault. They relied on precedent (HC Dilbagh Singh) to support recovery from the driver. "Order (Annexure P-3) has been rendered without referring to proviso to Section 146 of the Act..." The Court held that exemption from insurance under Section 146(3) is conditional upon establishment and maintenance of a fund; failure to have such a fund is a statutory violation by the State. The Court further held that "in absence of indictment in departmental proceedings, impugned order (Annexure P-3) loses its teeth, rendering it to be of no legal significance," and that an MACT award "standing alone... would not constitute the basis for recovery from the petitioner" without a proper departmental inquiry giving effective opportunity of being heard. Finally the Court emphasized that the Motor Vehicles Act is "beneficial legislation" where strict proof of guilt is not the object, but that does not absolve the employer from following required inhouse/disciplinary procedure before fixing liability on an employee. Writ petition allowed; order dated 24.2.1994 (Annexure P-3) directing recovery of Rs.3,10,427.95 from the petitioner is quashed. The Court directed the State to insure vehicles or establish and maintain the statutory fund under Section 146 and to follow departmental procedure before effecting recoveries. This is a Punjab & Haryana High Court writ challenging the State's order recovering MACT-ordered compensation from a bus driver. The MACT had found the driver negligent and awarded Rs.3,10,427.95; the State sought to recover that sum from the driver without holding an effective departmental inquiry or establishing the fund required by Section 146. The Court quashed the recovery order, holding that an MACT award alone cannot be the basis for recovering from an employee without following departmental procedure and that the State must comply with Section 146 conditions. Court relied on State of Maharashtra v. Shirke and HC authority Dilbagh Singh; held that exemption under s.146(3) requires establishment of a fund, MACT award alone cannot justify recovery from employee without an inquiry, and quashed the 24.2.1994 recovery order (Annexure P-3). Outcome: recovery order against driver quashed; MACT award amount Rs.3,10,427.95 remains the compensation figure; Court directed State to insure vehicles or establish the statutory fund.
